# AI Research Synthesis for Movemental’s Audience (April 2026)

**Audience (why this selection matters):** Leaders and teams in **mission-driven organizations**—nonprofits, churches and movements, seminaries, networks, and institutional partners—who must reconcile **speed and stewardship**, **individual productivity with organizational integrity**, and **vendor hype with formation**. This note prioritizes **primary or widely cited secondary sources**, flags **what is measured**, and separates **different studies** that are often conflated under loose “MIT” or “95%” headlines.

**How to use this:** Evidence for narrative, pricing, methodology pages, and “finding guidance worth trusting.” It is **not** legal or investment advice.

---

## 1. Executive tension (the headline reality)

Three truths now coexist in reputable sources:

1. **Generative AI spread faster than PCs or the internet** in population-adoption terms (Stanford HAI AI Index, 2026).
2. **Most enterprise-scale deployments still fail to convert pilots into measurable P&L outcomes**—the MIT-affiliated **GenAI Divide** analysis (2025) is the strongest recent articulation of this “divide.”
3. **Where tasks fit model strengths, rigorous field experiments show large gains**; **where tasks sit outside current capability, AI can hurt accuracy**—the **“jagged technological frontier”** finding from the HBS / MIT / BCG randomized experiment (2023).

For mission organizations, the practical implication is not “ignore AI” or “panic.” It is: **adoption is everywhere; transformation is not; and competence is task-contingent.** That aligns with Movemental’s emphasis on **sequence** (safety → sandbox → skills → solutions), **guides over posture**, and **formation over funnel**.

---

## 2. Tier A — The MIT “95%” finding (pilots, ROI, and the GenAI Divide)

### What it actually says

Press summaries (e.g. Fortune, August 2025; Computing UK; GlobeNewswire summaries) describe a July/August 2025 MIT-associated report titled **“The GenAI Divide: State of AI in Business 2025,”** led by authors including **Aditya Challapally** (Connected AI / MIT Media Lab context in secondary coverage). The reported headline statistic is that **roughly 95% of corporate generative AI pilots fail to deliver meaningful financial returns**, while a small minority capture disproportionate value—“the GenAI Divide.”

Secondary reporting attributes methods along these lines (exact **n** varies by outlet): **on the order of ~150 executive interviews**, **surveys of hundreds of employees or leaders**, and **review of hundreds of public AI deployments**—treat **n** as **approximate** until you cite the PDF directly.

### What it does *not* say

- It is **not** the same study as the **BCG / HBS “jagged frontier”** experiment below (different question, different methodology).
- It is **not** proof that “AI doesn’t work”; it is evidence that **enterprise integration, workflow fit, learning systems, and governance** dominate outcomes once baseline models are “good enough.”

### Why it matters for mission orgs

- Validates **skepticism of vendor-led pilot theater** without denying individual usefulness of ChatGPT-class tools.
- Supports the case for **back-office and operations-first** ROI (several summaries note stronger returns when automation aligns with process reality vs. marketing-heavy budgets)—parallel to **finance, HR, compliance, and program ops** in nonprofits.
- Reinforces **line-leader ownership**: tools that don’t embed in daily workflows stall; **central AI teams alone** rarely fix mission-critical adoption.

**Primary / stable references to chase:**

- Full report PDF (hosted in association with the study): [State of AI in Business 2025 report PDF](https://mlq.ai/media/quarterly_decks/v0.1_State_of_AI_in_Business_2025_Report.pdf)  
- Trade coverage with methodology sketches: [Computing UK — MIT report on pilot failure rates](https://www.computing.co.uk/news/2025/ai/mit-report-95pc-corporate-generative-ai-pilots-fail)

---

## 3. Tier A — Stanford HAI *AI Index* (2026): speed of adoption vs. depth of transformation

Stanford’s **AI Index** is the closest thing the field has to an annual, cross-domain benchmark. The **2026** edition (released April 2026 per Stanford’s site) emphasizes:

- **Very high organizational uptake** of AI in business contexts (reporting cites **88%** of surveyed organizations using AI in at least one function—validate exact wording in the chapter PDF).
- **Generative AI** spread to a large share of organizations and, separately, **rapid consumer adoption curves** for generative tools (communications around **53%** population adoption within three years appear in Stanford’s public chapter summaries—again, confirm against the chapter text you cite publicly).
- **Uneven depth**: agentic deployment remains **early** across many functions (single-digit adoption in several areas per public summaries).
- **Economic and labor implications** tracked in the Economy chapter—investment growth, revenue concentration among AI suppliers, and workforce effects.

**Why it matters for mission orgs**

- Explains **board urgency** (“everyone’s using it”) alongside **staff skepticism** (“we see no institutional payoff”).
- Supports teaching leaders to distinguish **personal tool use** from **institutional capability**.

**References:**

- [The 2026 AI Index Report — Stanford HAI](https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2026-ai-index-report/)  
- [Economy chapter landing](https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2026-ai-index-report/economy)

---

## 4. Tier B — Field experiment: “Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier” (HBS / MIT / BCG, 2023)

This is the peer-style working paper often summarized as “the BCG consultant study.” **Authors include MIT Sloan and Harvard researchers**; **Boston Consulting Group** partnered on execution.

**Full title:** *Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of AI on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality* (Harvard Business School Technology & Operations Working Paper No. 24-013, Sept 2023; SSRN).

### Core quantitative findings (inside vs. outside the frontier)

On **18 realistic consulting tasks judged “inside” current GPT-4 capability**:

- **+12.2%** more tasks completed  
- **25.1%** faster completion  
- **>40%** higher quality vs. control  
- Largest lifts for **below-median performers** (**~43%** vs. baseline) vs. **~17%** for above-median  

On **one task deliberately constructed outside** the frontier:

- Consultants with AI were **19 percentage points less likely** to be correct than those without—showing **harm**, not just absence of benefit.

### Concepts mission leaders can reuse

- **Jagged frontier:** tasks that *look* similar can fall on opposite sides of what models handle well.
- **Centaur vs. Cyborg patterns:** successful users either **split** human vs. machine labor deliberately or **interleave** tightly—both require **literacy and judgment**, not licenses alone.

**Reference:**

- [SSRN abstract page](https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4573321)

---

## 5. Tier B — BCG follow-on (2024): GenAI expanding *beyond* current skillsets

BCG’s **second** major experiment (480 consultants; comparison to specialist benchmarks) examines whether GenAI helps workers complete **tasks outside their prior expertise** (e.g., data-science-style tasks). Press materials (Sept 2025) summarize large **skill-stretch** effects in some dimensions (e.g., coding-adjacent tasks) but also stress **failure modes** when the tool misleads on statistical reliability—consistent with the **frontier** lesson.

**Why it matters:** Mission orgs often ask junior staff to “do AI” without senior analytical oversight; **stretch** can create **phantom expertise**.

**Reference:**

- [BCG press — GenAI elevates aptitude on complex tasks](https://www.bcg.com/press/5september2024-generative-ai-knowledge-workers-consultants)  
- [BCG publication hub — productivity & capabilities](https://www.bcg.com/publications/2024/gen-ai-increases-productivity-and-expands-capabilities)

---

## 6. Tier B — McKinsey: economic upside vs. short-run ROI frustration

McKinsey’s long-running **“economic potential of generative AI”** work (2023 and updates) sizes **trillions** in possible value and describes broad **automation potential** for **work activities** (time-use framing—not “percent of jobs eliminated” in a simplistic sense). Separately, **workplace surveys** (e.g., “Superagency” / AI-in-the-workplace reports for 2025) document:

- High **planned investment** in AI  
- **Very few** organizations self-reporting **mature**, workflow-integrated deployments (**~1%** “mature” in some 2025 survey framings—confirm before quoting narrowly)  
- **Limited** reported revenue impact from gen AI to date for many respondents, vs. optimism about the next years  

**Why it matters:** Gives **quantitative language for boards** while supporting a **pro-trust, anti-hype** stance: potential is real; **capture is selective**.

**References:**

- [The economic potential of generative AI — McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/mckinsey-digital/our-insights/the-economic-potential-of-generative-AI-the-next-productivity-frontier)  
- [Superagency in the workplace — McKinsey](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/superagency-in-the-workplace-empowering-people-to-unlock-ais-full-potential-at-work)  
- [The State of AI — Global Survey](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/quantumblack/our-insights/the-state-of-ai)

---

## 7. Tier C — Nonprofit-specific benchmarks (capacity, strategy, governance gaps)

### TechSoup × Tapp Network — *State of AI in Nonprofits: 2025*

Large-N sector survey (**1,321+** respondents in public materials) highlights:

- **Many still exploring**; a significant share **not yet using** AI.  
- **Low single-digit** share reporting **successful** operational/mission integration in some framings (**~7%** in benchmark language—verify in PDF before hard claims).  
- **Strategic gap:** high exploratory interest vs. **low share** with a **clear, communicated AI strategy** (blog summaries cite **~85% exploring** but only **~24%** with a formal strategy—confirm in source).  
- **Resource concentration:** larger orgs adopt faster; small shops depend on **1–2** overloaded decision-makers.

**Reference:**

- [Tapp Network announcement / summary](https://blog.tappnetwork.com/tapp-and-techsoup-release-2025-ai-benchmark-report)

### NTEN — *2025 Data Empowerment Report*

Nonprofit **data governance** survey (**220+** organizations) finds:

- **AI-specific data policies** are among the **least implemented**—nearly **half** **no** dedicated policy in some framings.  
- **“Adopting AI tools”** jumped into **top operational priorities** alongside training and impact evaluation.

**Reference:**

- [NTEN Data Empowerment Report PDF](https://word.nten.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/07/2025-Data-Empowerment-Report.pdf)

**Movemental tie-in:** Nonprofits often **lack policies before adoption**—exactly where **Safety → Sandbox** sequencing matters.

---

## 8. Tier C — Reliability, hallucinations, and “why confident wrong answers persist”

### OpenAI research — *Why Language Models Hallucinate* (Sept 2025)

Peer-facing research argues hallucinations are **partly incentivized**: standard **accuracy-based** benchmarks reward **guessing** over **calibrated uncertainty**, so models inherit **test-taking** behavior. Mitigation may require **changing evaluation incentives**, not only adding side metrics.

**References:**

- [OpenAI research post](https://openai.com/research/why-language-models-hallucinate)  
- [PDF — Why language models hallucinate](https://cdn.openai.com/pdf/d04913be-3f6f-4d2b-b283-ff432ef4aaa5/why-language-models-hallucinate.pdf)

### Deployment reality

OpenAI’s own **system cards** for reasoning models (e.g., o-series) discuss **hallucination rates** on factuality-style suites and tradeoffs between **verbosity / accuracy / erroneous claims**. Third-party commentary varies; **trust vendor evals as directional**, not gospel.

**Why it matters for faith and mission contexts:** **Trust models for pastoral or counseling tone** at your peril when **epistemic humility** is doctrinally central.

---

## 9. Ordered significance for *this* moment (April 2026)

| Rank | Finding | Source cluster | Movemental implication |
|------|---------|----------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Pilot-to-scale failure / GenAI Divide** — most enterprise initiatives don’t convert to durable ROI | MIT-associated **GenAI Divide** report + reporting | Validates **order matters** and **guide-led integration** vs. tool shopping |
| 2 | **Adoption breadth vs. depth** — ubiquitous tools, shallow institutional embedding | Stanford **AI Index 2026**, McKinsey surveys | Frames **why boards feel behind** while **staff feel unchanged** |
| 3 | **Jagged frontier** — AI helps **inside** capability; **hurts** outside | Dell’Anna et al., HBS Working Paper w/ BCG | Mandates **task literacy** and **human verification** in ministry communications, counseling-adjacent workflows, grant narratives |
| 4 | **Nonprofit strategy & policy gaps** | TechSoup/Tapp, NTEN | **Governance-first** wins trust with donors and communities |
| 5 | **Hallucination incentives** | OpenAI 2025 paper | Supports **truthfulness, citation, and uncertainty** norms in Christian / institutional ethics |

---

## 10. Methods discipline (avoid embarrassing conflations)

- **“MIT 95%”** in recent press almost always refers to **GenAI Divide / pilot ROI** reporting (2025), **not** the 2023 **productivity uplift** figures from the **jagged frontier** paper.  
- **BCG study numbers** (~12%, ~25%, ~40%) come from **controlled tasks**, not from “95% of all AI projects.”  
- When citing **percentages**, prefer **primary PDFs** or **publisher tables**; surveys differ by **population** (global execs vs. US-only vs. nonprofit).

---
